title: Cooling systems, devices, and related methods
abstract: Cooling systems are described herein that may be used in connection with one or more attached devices to cool patient tissue. The disclosed cooling systems include a refrigeration unit containing a thermoelectric element in thermal communication with a heat exchanger, a fluid pump in fluid communication with a fluid inlet and a fluid outlet, tubing connecting the fluid inlet to the fluid outlet, a fluid cooling element in thermal contact with the thermoelectric element, and a temperature sensor positioned to detect a temperature of fluid within the tubing. The temperature of fluid within the tubing can be controlled by a control unit having a user interface and a power controller to adjust cooling power to the thermoelectric element. Various types of devices can be configured to receive and circulate cooled fluid from the cooling systems, such as retractor blades, retractor shims, cooling pads, and scope sheaths.
claims:
Claims 1. A self-contained cooling system comprising: a housing containing a thermoelectric element in thermal communication with a heat exchanger; a fluid pump in fluid communication with a fluid inlet and a fluid outlet; tubing positioned within the housing, wherein the tubing connects the fluid inlet to the fluid outlet; a fluid cooling element in thermal contact with the thermoelectric element; a fan; a power cable in electrical connection with the thermoelectric element; a temperature sensor positioned to detect a temperature of fluid within the tubing; a power control capable of adjusting cooling power to the thermoelectric element; a power switch to supply or deprive the thermoelectric element of power; a power fdter to filter power supplied by the power cable, an AC to DC power supply; a fluid cable in fluid communication with the fluid inlet and the fluid outlet; a fluid connector to the patient cooling device; and a user interface capable of displaying the detected temperature of the fluid. 2. The self-contained cooling system of claim 1, wherein the fluid cable is connectable to a patient cooling device. 3. The self-contained cooling system of claim 2, wherein the patient cooling device is a retractor blade with internal fluid paths. 4. The self-contained cooling system of claim 2, wherein the patient cooling device is a bone screw with internal fluid paths. 5. The self-contained cooling system of claim 2, wherein the patient cooling device is a tissue cooling pad with internal fluid paths. 6. The self-contained cooling system of claim 1, wherein the fluid cooling element is in fluid contact with fluid within the tubing. 7. The self-contained cooling system of claim 1, wherein the fluid is a sterile liquid. 8. A cooling system comprising: a refrigeration unit housing containing: a thermoelectric element in thermal communication with a heat exchanger; a fluid pump in fluid communication with a fluid inlet and a fluid outlet; tubing positioned within the refrigeration unit housing, wherein the tubing connects the fluid inlet to the fluid outlet; a fluid cooling element in thermal contact with the thermoelectric element; a fan; a temperature sensor positioned to detect a temperature of fluid within the tubing; a power and signal connector to the control unit housing; and a user interface; and a control unit housing containing: a power connector; a power and signal connector; a power controller to adjust cooling power to the thermoelectric element; and a power switch. 9. The cooling system of claim 8, wherein the fluid cable is connectable to a patient cooling device. 10. The cooling system of claim 9, wherein the patient cooling device is a retractor blade with internal fluid paths. 11. The cooling system of claim 9, wherein the patient cooling device is a bone screw with internal fluid paths. 12. The cooling system of claim 9, wherein the patient cooling device is a tissue cooling pad with internal fluid paths. 13. The cooling system of claim 8, wherein the fluid cooling element is in fluid contact with fluid within the tubing. 14. The cooling system of claim 8, wherein the fluid is a sterile liquid. 15. A method of applying thermal therapy to a target area of a patient tissue during surgery using the self-contained cooling system of claim 1, the method comprising: connecting a power source to the self-contained sterile refrigeration unit; connecting a tubing kit and a thermal therapy device to create a closed-loop circuit; filling the tubing kit and the thermal therapy device with a sterile fluid; placing the thermal therapy device in contact with the target area of patient tissue; and activating the self-contained cooling system to circulate the sterile fluid through the thermal therapy device and the tubing kit to cool the target area of patient tissue. 16. A method of applying thermal therapy to a target area of a patient tissue during surgery using the cooling system of claim 8, the method comprising: connecting a power source to the cooling system; connecting a tubing kit and a thermal therapy device to create a closed-loop circuit; filling the tubing kit and the thermal therapy device with a sterile fluid; 

 placing the thermal therapy device in contact with the target area of patient tissue; and activating the cooling system to circulate the sterile fluid through the thermal therapy device and the tubing kit to cool the target area of patient tissue. 17. A method of filling a cooling system to cool patient tissue during surgery, the method comprising: connecting a power source to the cooling system; connecting a patient cooling device coupler to a cooling system fluid tubing coupler; connecting a port on the coupler to a liquid source; connecting a port on the coupler to a disposal vessel; initiating a fill state in the control system; manually or automatically terminating the fill state; disconnecting of both ports to the liquid source and a disposal vessel; securely closing caps on both ports; placing the cooling device at a target tissue site; selecting a target cooling device temperature; and initiating cooling of patient tissue.
